one_line_takeaway: A GPT-4o + LangChain hierarchical multi-agent pentesting tool (AutoPentest) achieves high autonomy on black-box HTB targets but only completes 15-26% of subtasks -- roughly matching manual ChatGPT-4o -- because it enumerates well yet still fails at multi-step exploitation.
key_finding: AutoPentest and the manual ChatGPT-4o baseline performed nearly identically, both completing only 15-26% of subtasks per machine. AutoPentest edged out the baseline on just one machine (Codify: 7 vs 6 subtasks) and tied on the other two. The recurring qualitative result is that the agents enumerate targets and identify vulnerabilities reasonably well, but reliably FAIL at the multi-step exploitation needed to actually chain a foothold into privilege escalation. AutoPentest's real advantage over the baseline is autonomy (little human interaction) and scalability, not raw success rate. Cost was materially higher than the baseline: $96.20 total across AutoPentest runs vs a $20/month ChatGPT Plus subscription, though AutoPentest can issue far more queries per hour.
core_contribution: AutoPentest, an open-source application (single-author, Julius Henke) that performs black-box penetration testing with a high degree of autonomy by integrating OpenAI's GPT-4o with the LangChain/LangGraph agent framework. Its architecture is a hierarchical multi-agent system: a deterministic service-discovery stage (nmap for port/service/OS enumeration plus NIST NVD lookups mapping discovered CPEs to known CVEs) feeds a Planner agent that builds and re-plans a high-level attack plan; a Supervisor agent delegates each step to one of eight Specialised Workers. The workers are organized around the OWASP Top 10 (2021) plus a Privilege Escalation worker (Enumeration, Broken Access Control, Cryptographic Failures, Injection, Insecure Design, Security Configuration, Identification and Authentication Failures, Privilege Escalation). Each worker is augmented with RAG over a Pinecone vector DB (one namespace per worker, text-embedding-ada-002 embeddings, cosine similarity) populated from manually curated sources (OWASP, MITRE CWE, portswigger.net, hacktricks, golinuxcloud, github). Workers wield tools: Tavily LLM search, temporary/persistent/reverse shells, a Python execution environment, Playwright headless-Chromium browser automation, and NIST NVD query tools (Enumeration worker only). A human-in-the-loop role is minimal: supply the target IP and optionally approve/deny shell commands for safety.
threat_model: Offensive-security automation (dual-use). AutoPentest embodies an external, black-box attacker who is given only a target IP/domain and no prior knowledge of the system, then autonomously drives the full chain of reconnaissance, enumeration, vulnerability identification, exploitation, and privilege escalation against CTF-style targets. Intended use is authorized penetration testing / vulnerability management; the paper explicitly acknowledges the dual-use risk that the same capability aids malicious actors, and argues for open release on the grounds that attackers could build equivalents anyway. Safety controls are lightweight: optional human command review (default on) to block commands aimed at the wrong host or denial-of-service, plus HTB terms-of-service compliance and private VPN instances during experiments.
limitations: Very small and easy evaluation: only 3 HTB machines, all rated 'easy' and all Linux -- no hard targets, no Windows/AD, no white-box scenarios. Only a single LLM (GPT-4o) evaluated; no comparison across models (Gemini, Claude, Llama, etc.) for AutoPentest itself. Scores are from the 'best run' per 2-hour window rather than an averaged multi-run success rate, and LLM non-determinism across runs is not statistically characterized. Exploitation is the clear weak point -- correct next subtask often identified but not executed well enough to land the exploit; interactive tooling like msfconsole was handled poorly. Autonomy is undermined in practice by three failure modes: workers looping on a task without reporting back (task repetition), workers falsely assuming an active shell context on the target, and workers hitting the 100-iteration cap or uncaught errors and returning no observations. Cost is volatile and driven by outliers: one Devvortex run alone cost $41.10 (~43% of the total) because a worker failed to report back and ballooned the context/token count. The human safety-review step could in principle bias runs; mitigated only by discipline in rejecting solely for safety reasons. Baseline is a single-author manual-ChatGPT comparison, not a comparison against other published autonomous pentesting agents on a shared benchmark (the paper itself notes the field lacks a common benchmark).

datasets_used: Hack The Box machine Devvortex (easy, Linux, released Nov 2023; 26 solution subtasks)
Hack The Box machine Broker (easy, Linux, released Nov 2023; 10 solution subtasks)
Hack The Box machine Codify (easy, Linux, released Nov 2023; 27 solution subtasks)
RAG corpus manually curated from OWASP Top 10 (2021) descriptions, MITRE CWE, portswigger.net, book.hacktricks.xyz, golinuxcloud.com, github.com
NIST NVD queried at runtime for CVEs matching nmap-discovered CPEs
quantitative_results: Devvortex: AutoPentest 4/26 subtasks (15.38%), ChatGPT-4o baseline 4/26 (15.38%) -- tie
Broker: AutoPentest 2/10 subtasks (20.00%), ChatGPT-4o baseline 2/10 (20.00%) -- tie
Codify: AutoPentest 7/27 subtasks (25.93%), ChatGPT-4o baseline 6/27 (22.22%) -- AutoPentest +1
Overall both approaches stayed below 30% subtask completion on every machine
Total AutoPentest LLM cost across all runs: $96.20; average per run $9.62; median per run $6.43
Total tokens across AutoPentest runs: 18,718K input + 174K output
Single outlier Devvortex run: 8,186K input / 11K output tokens = $41.10 (~43% of total cost)
Azure pricing at experiment time: $0.005 per 1K input tokens, $0.015 per 1K output tokens
ChatGPT Plus baseline cost: $20 for one month (80 GPT-4o messages/hour cap)
Scalability: Azure 450K tokens/min limit implies >=210 queries/hour vs 80/hour for ChatGPT Plus
AutoPentest configured at LLM temperature 0; worker iteration cap = 100; shell output truncated to first+last 3,000 chars above 30,000 chars
baselines_compared: Primary baseline: human manually operating the ChatGPT-4o web UI (copy/paste commands and outputs) on the same three HTB machines

Contextual (related-work, NOT re-run by this paper): Fang et al. HPTSA reported 53% success with GPT-4-Turbo on 15 CVEs and $4.39 avg run cost; Happe et al. Wintermute solved 75-100% of Linux privesc cases with GPT-4; Moskal et al. 60% (6/10 services) on Metasploitable 2 with GPT-3.5 -- all cited as comparison points with narrower scope, not as AutoPentest's own runs
